title: EIP-8151: Private Key Deactivation Aware ecRecover
description: Modifies the ecRecover precompile to respect EIP-7851 key deactivation. After ECDSA public key recovery, the precompile checks whether the recovered address has a deactivated private key. If so, it returns 32 zero bytes (the existing failure sentinel) instead of the recovered address. Gas costs follow EIP-2929 warm/cold rules for the additional state access.
author: Liyi Guo (@colinlyguo), Nicolas Consigny (@nconsigny)
laymanDescription: When an account owner deactivates their private key (via EIP-7851), existing immutable contracts still accept signatures from that key. This change makes the built-in signature checker automatically reject deactivated keys, so deployed contracts like token permit functions respect key deactivation without needing updates.

endUsers.description: Users who deactivate their private keys gain stronger protection since immutable contracts using ecRecover will automatically reject signatures from deactivated keys.

appDevs.description: No changes needed for contracts already checking ecRecover for zero return. Contracts performing application-level ECDSA recovery bypass this change and remain unaffected.

walletDevs.description: Wallets may surface key deactivation status more prominently; no protocol-level integration changes required beyond awareness of the new behavior.

toolingInfra.description: Debugging and simulation tools must account for ecRecover now performing a state read, affecting gas estimation and trace output for successful recoveries.

layer2s.description: L2 fault proof systems that cache ecRecover results as pure functions must update, since the precompile now depends on account state.

stakersNodes.description: No direct impact on validators or staking operations; the change only affects ecRecover precompile behavior.

clClients.description: No consensus client changes required; this EIP modifies only the execution layer ecRecover precompile.

elClients.description: Must modify the ecRecover precompile to read account code after recovery and add EIP-2929 warm/cold gas accounting for the state access.

benefits: Protects users with deactivated keys from unauthorized permit and signature-based actions on immutable contracts.
Requires no changes to deployed contracts; existing zero-return checks naturally reject deactivated keys.
Uses the existing ecRecover failure sentinel, preserving backward compatibility for well-written contracts.
Follows established EIP-2929 gas accounting for the new state access, avoiding novel gas models.
tradeoffs: Every successful ecRecover call now incurs additional gas for the account state read.
Breaks the assumption that ecRecover is a pure function, impacting L2 fault proof systems.
Key deactivation is per-chain state, so deactivation on one chain does not protect across other EVM chains.

improveUX.description: Ensures that key deactivation via EIP-7851 is enforced across immutable contracts using ecRecover, closing a gap where deactivated keys could still authorize actions through permit-style functions.
